question: How do you handle disagreements with team members about technical decisions?
shortAnswer: I separate the decision from the person. I present data, ask questions to understand their perspective, and propose a time-boxed experiment if we cannot agree. In SGN, when we disagreed on caching strategy, I built a prototype of both approaches and measured — data ended the debate.
detailedAnswer:
Technical disagreements are healthy — they mean people care. The problem is when they become personal or block progress.

My approach:
1. Listen fully before responding (not just waiting for my turn)
2. Ask "what problem are you solving?" — often we agree on the problem but differ on the solution
3. Identify the reversibility — if the decision is easily reversible, just pick one and move on
4. For irreversible decisions, propose a time-boxed spike or prototype
5. If still stuck, defer to whoever will maintain the code long-term

The key insight: being right matters less than shipping. A good-enough solution today beats a perfect solution debated for a week.
keyPoints: Separate the person from the idea
Data over opinions — prototype and measure
Defer to maintainer when equal arguments
Being right matters less than making progress
redFlags: "I just do what they say" — shows no technical ownership
Never compromising — always insisting on your way

followUps.question: What if your manager disagrees with you?
followUps.hint: Show that you voice your concerns clearly but commit once a decision is made.

followUps.question: Tell me about a time you were wrong in a technical decision.

realExperience.title: Caching strategy debate in SGN3
realExperience.description: Disagreed with colleague about Infinispan vs application-level cache. Built both prototypes in 2 hours, measured hit rates. Application cache won for our use case. No hard feelings.

question: How do you prioritize when everything is urgent?
shortAnswer: I use impact×reversibility as the filter. What breaks production gets fixed now. What blocks other people is next. What only affects me waits. I communicate priorities transparently — stakeholders know what is queued and why.
detailedAnswer:
When everything feels urgent, the real skill is distinguishing between urgent and important.

My framework:
1. Production incidents → immediate (users are affected NOW)
2. Blockers for teammates → same day (multiplier effect)
3. Deadline-driven work → based on actual deadline, not perceived urgency
4. Everything else → batched into focus blocks

The mistake I see: treating every Slack message as P0. If everything is urgent, nothing is. I protect focus time and communicate clearly: "I see your request. I will address it after X, expected by Y."
keyPoints: Protect focus time — not everything is P0
redFlags: "I just work harder" — shows no strategic thinking
Never saying no or pushing back on scope

followUps.question: What do you do when your manager adds something urgent to an already full sprint?
followUps.hint: Trade-off: something else must move. Make the trade-off visible.

followUps.question: How do you handle interruptions during deep work?
followUps.hint: Batch interruptions, use async communication, set expectations.

realExperience.title: Balancing production incidents with project delivery
realExperience.description: Managed a week where 3 production bugs hit while I was implementing a major feature. Communicated clearly to PM which deadlines would slip and why. Fixed critical bugs first, delivered feature 2 days late with full quality.

question: What does "senior" mean to you?
shortAnswer: A senior solves ambiguous problems independently, multiplies the team (code reviews, mentoring, documentation), owns decisions and their consequences, and knows when NOT to build something. Seniority is about judgment, not years.
detailedAnswer:
Seniority is not about knowing more APIs or having more years of experience. It is about judgment.

A senior developer:
- Navigates ambiguity without asking for step-by-step instructions
- Makes the team better (reviews, mentoring, patterns, documentation)
- Owns outcomes, not just code ("the feature works" vs "the business problem is solved")
- Knows when to say "we should NOT build this" — the most valuable code is the code not written
- Communicates proactively — bad news early, trade-offs explicitly
- Makes reversible decisions quickly and irreversible decisions carefully

What it is NOT:
- Knowing every framework
- Working alone without help
- Being the fastest coder
- Having 10+ years of experience
redFlags: "A senior works alone without needing help"
Cannot articulate how they help their team beyond writing code

followUps.question: How do you multiply your team?
followUps.hint: Code reviews, knowledge sharing, documentation, pairing, improving processes.

followUps.question: Give an example of something you decided NOT to build.
followUps.hint: Shows restraint and judgment — YAGNI in practice.

realExperience.title: Refused to build a custom report engine
realExperience.description: Stakeholder wanted a configurable report system. Showed that Jasper + 3 parameterized templates solved 90% of cases. Saved 2 months of development. Sometimes the best code is the code you do not write.
